questions.question: Which of the following is a valid type for a switch expression in Java?
What keyword is used to exit a switch case and prevent fall-through?
What happens if you omit all break statements in a switch block and a case matches?
options: The switch block is skipped entirely
Execution falls through all remaining cases including default
questions.answer: Execution falls through all remaining cases including default

questions.question: Which of the following types is NOT supported by a switch expression?
What is the purpose of the default case in a switch statement?
options: It must always be the first case listed
It executes when no other case value matches the switch expression
It is required for the switch statement to compile
questions.answer: It executes when no other case value matches the switch expression

questions.question: Which statement about String comparison in a switch is true?
options: String switch comparisons use the == operator internally
String values cannot be used in switch statements

questions.question: When is a switch statement generally preferred over an if-else-if chain?
options: When you need to evaluate range comparisons
When you are testing a single variable against multiple discrete constant values
When you need to evaluate compound boolean expressions
When the condition involves relational operators like < or >
questions.answer: When you are testing a single variable against multiple discrete constant values

questions.question: Can the default case be placed anywhere inside a switch block?
options: No, it must always be the last case
No, it must always be the first case
Yes, it can appear at any position in the switch block
Yes, but only if there are no other cases
questions.answer: Yes, it can appear at any position in the switch block

questions.question: Which of the following will cause a compilation error when used as a case label?
options: A final int variable initialized with a literal

questions.question: Why must case labels in a switch statement be compile-time constants?
options: So the switch block can be evaluated at runtime only once
Because Java does not support variables
So the compiler can verify that no two cases have the same value and can optimize the jump table
Because case labels must always be String literals
questions.answer: So the compiler can verify that no two cases have the same value and can optimize the jump table

questions.question: What is an example of intentional fall-through in a switch statement?
options: Forgetting to add a break and printing extra output by mistake
Grouping multiple case labels together so they share the same code block
Using default as the first case
Using a return statement instead of break
questions.answer: Grouping multiple case labels together so they share the same code block

questions.question: Which scenario is better handled by if-else-if rather than a switch statement?
options: Mapping a menu selection number to an action
Classifying a numeric score into letter grade ranges (0-59, 60-69, etc.)
Converting a day number (1-7) to a day name
Matching an operator character to an arithmetic operation
questions.answer: Classifying a numeric score into letter grade ranges (0-59, 60-69, etc.)

questions.question: Why can't a switch statement easily handle range comparisons like score >= 90?
options: Because switch does not support the int type
Because case labels must be exact constant values, not boolean expressions or ranges
Because switch can only be used with String values
Because break statements prevent range checking
questions.answer: Because case labels must be exact constant values, not boolean expressions or ranges

questions.question: How should you handle user input to ensure case-insensitive matching in a String switch?
options: Use == instead of .equals() in the case labels
Convert the input to a consistent case using toLowerCase() or toUpperCase() before the switch
Add duplicate case labels for every possible casing
Use a default case that matches all variations
questions.answer: Convert the input to a consistent case using toLowerCase() or toUpperCase() before the switch

questions.question: Since which version of Java has String been supported as a switch expression type?

options: Intentional fall-through to group cases sharing the same logic
A compilation error because multiple cases are on one line
An example of why switch cannot handle multiple values
questions.answer: Intentional fall-through to group cases sharing the same logic

options: No, because CONSTANT is not a literal
No, because switch does not support int
Yes, because CONSTANT is a final variable initialized with a compile-time constant
Yes, but only if CONSTANT is declared static
questions.answer: Yes, because CONSTANT is a final variable initialized with a compile-time constant

questions.question: Why can't a switch statement handle compound boolean conditions like (hasTicket && hasID)?
options: Because boolean is not a supported type for switch expressions
Because switch only supports String expressions
Because break statements interfere with boolean evaluation
Because the default case overrides boolean logic
questions.answer: Because boolean is not a supported type for switch expressions

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid switch expression type?
